summary: Socrates was an ancient Greek philosopher who laid the groundwork for Western logic and philosophy. The text outlines his life in Athens, his emphasis on self-knowledge and the mind over the body, his development of the Socratic method, and his trial and execution for challenging the status quo.

principles.statement: Ultimate wisdom comes from knowing oneself.
principles.rationale: The more a person knows, the greater their ability to reason and make choices that will bring true happiness.

principles.statement: Philosophy should achieve practical results for the greater well-being of society.
principles.rationale: Ethical systems should be based on human reason rather than theological doctrine to help people make choices motivated by the desire for happiness.

principles.statement: The mind is vastly more important than the human body.
principles.rationale: Physical ideals and beauty are relatively unimportant compared to brilliant debates, penetrating thought, and intellectual development.

principles.statement: True wisdom is recognizing one's own ignorance.
principles.rationale: Claiming to have no ideas prevents arrogance and opens the mind to seeking actual political and ethical truths.

frameworks.name: Socratic Method
frameworks.when_to_apply: When seeking to arrive at political and ethical truths, or when challenging conventional wisdom.
steps: Adopt a position of ignorance, refraining from lecturing about what you know.
Ask questions of your audience or opponent in a dialectic manner.
Compel the audience to think through the problem step-by-step.
Arrive at a logical conclusion that often reveals the obvious answer or exposes the opponent's foolishness.

mental_models.name: The Gadfly
mental_models.description: A role assumed by an individual to provide a vital service to their community by continually questioning and challenging the status quo and its defenders, preventing societal complacency.
mental_models.example: Socrates used this model to defend himself in court, arguing that his relentless questioning of the Athenian elite was not a crime, but a necessary public service.

heuristics: Recognize your own ignorance to achieve true wisdom.

anti_patterns.description: Fixating on physical beauty, wealth, and past glories.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It distracts from the greater importance of the mind and prevents the intellectual development necessary for true happiness and societal stability.

anti_patterns.description: Governing a society through tyranny or democracy.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Government works best when ruled by individuals who have the greatest ability, knowledge, virtue, and a complete understanding of themselves, rather than by a tyrant or the general masses.

anti_patterns.description: Basing ethical systems on theological doctrine.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Ethics should instead be established on human reason to achieve practical results for the well-being of society.
